narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a paleontologist who named a volcano
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather John Strong Newberry was a leading 19th-century American paleontologist and paleobotanist on the Columbia University School of Mines faculty for 24 years; he helped found the Geological Society of America. Two species — Eucyclogobius newberryi (goby) and Salmo newberryi (rainbow trout) — and Lake Newberry NY and Newberry Volcano in Oregon all bear his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a paleontologist who named a volcano
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather John Strong Newberry was a leading 19th-century American paleontologist and paleobotanist on the Columbia University School of Mines faculty for 24 years; he helped found the Geological Society of America. Two species — Eucyclogobius newberryi (goby) and Salmo newberryi (rainbow trout) — and Lake Newberry NY and Newberry Volcano in Oregon all bear his name.

cross_connections.description: Grandson and successor editor of the Springfield Republican — paper passed from grandfather to grandson through Sally Bowles + Thomas Hooker marriage.

cross_connections.display_label: Earlier abolitionist editor of The Emancipator from same Hooker tree

narrative_blocks.body: He was named for his paternal grandfather Rev. Richard Hooker, the Macon Georgia Presbyterian missionary who died at forty-nine in 1857. He graduated Yale in 1899 the same year his elder sister Aurelia died, and lived to age 89 in 1967.

narrative_blocks.header: Took over Springfield Republican from cousin Sam V
narrative_blocks.body: His maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III had built the Springfield Republican into one of America's most influential newspapers from 1844 to 1878. When the fifth Samuel Bowles broke the three-generation editor pattern by not running the paper, Richard — his cousin and the son of Sally Bowles Hooker — took over as editor and general manager, carrying the newspaper from the Bowles family into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Augusta Georgia, reared in New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: His Macon-born father Thomas Hooker was already settling toward a New Haven banking career when Richard was born at Augusta Georgia in 1878. The family soon settled at New Haven where Richard was reared and attended Yale, graduating with the class of 1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington correspondent before taking the Republican
narrative_blocks.body: After his year of European study he joined the Republican and served as its Washington correspondent from 1904 to 1911 — covering Roosevelt's late presidency and Taft's full term from the press galleries. He succeeded his grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor in 1915.

narrative_blocks.header: Friend of Wilson, declined two presidential offers
narrative_blocks.body: He was a friend of President Woodrow Wilson and strongly supported him editorially during World War I and in the fight for the League of Nations. He declined Wilson's offer of Assistant Secretary of the Navy and an earlier offer from President Taft to serve as press secretary.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Macmillan history of the Republican, 1924
narrative_blocks.body: He authored 'The Story of an Independent Newspaper' (Macmillan 1924), a history of his family's paper across nearly a century. He also wrote corporate histories of the Massachusetts Mutual and Aetna life insurance companies, drawing on his banking-family background.

He served as a director of the Associated Press from 1927 to 1934, near the peak of his Republican editorial career. After stepping down from the Republican Company presidency in 1932 he contributed editorials almost until his death at eighty-nine in his Longmeadow MA home in 1967.

education.notes: Year of study in Europe after Yale graduation.

career.notes: Succeeded his maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor 1915. (Per 1934 Time magazine jingle, the 5th Samuel Bowles broke the 3-generation editor pattern — Richard, the Bowles cousin via the Hooker marriage, took over.)
President of the Republican Company until 1932; contributed editorials almost until his 1967 death.

burial.plot_notes: Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford CT — funeral at Christ Episcopal Church Guilford

education.notes: Hawken School in Gates Mills, OH (the obit's 'Hawkins School in Gatesmills, OH' is a transcription of Hawken).
Yale Law 1938 — fourth straight generation in Hooker line at Yale (Pope Dwight; Tim V; HD0623 grandfather; father Richard Sr 1899; HD04884 himself JD 1938).

career.notes: Joined firm after Yale Law 1938; became partner. Firm dissolved in the 1980s.

career.organization: Thompson, Weir & Barclay → Murtha, Cullina, Richter & Pinney (merged)
career.notes: Became of counsel after Daggett Colby & Hooker dissolved; firm merged into Murtha Cullina; retired 1995.

institutions.blurb: Joined the firm 1938 after uncle Thomas Jr.'s 1936 death; preserved Hooker name as partner until 1980s dissolution.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding partner of Daggett, Colby & Hooker firm
narrative_blocks.body: After Williams 1935 and Yale Law 1938 he joined the New Haven law firm that would carry his name as Daggett, Colby & Hooker until its 1980s dissolution. He then went of counsel at Thompson Weir & Barclay, which later merged with Murtha Cullina Richter & Pinney; he retired 1995 after 57 years in law.

narrative_blocks.header: Society of Cincinnati member, fifty-seven year marriage
narrative_blocks.body: He held membership in the Society of the Cincinnati — the hereditary Revolutionary War officers' organization that his great-great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had also belonged to. He married Mildred Bakewell in 1941 and the marriage lasted 57 years to his 1998 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the firm his uncle had co-founded
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Thomas Hooker Jr. had been name partner at White, Daggett and Hooker (1910), Bristol and White (1913-1934), and co-founder of Daggett and Hooker (1934) before his 1936 death. When Richard Jr. graduated Yale Law in 1938 he was admitted to partnership at the renamed Daggett, Colby and Hooker — preserving the family name in the firm across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his great-grandfather Samuel Bowles editor
narrative_blocks.body: His middle name Bowles honored his maternal great-great-grandfather Samuel Bowles III — editor of the Springfield Republican 1844-1878. Born Cleveland OH 1925, he died Brooklyn NY at fifty-nine and was returned to the Longmeadow MA family cemetery for burial.

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — alongside her husband Dr. Ralph E. Knutti

marriages.officiant: Rev. Edwin Crocker, Blandford Congregational Church (at the Hooker summer home)

education.school_name: Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine

career.location: Rochester NY → Chicago IL → Appalachia region

career.role: Branch chief and Assistant Director, Office of Program Planning and Evaluation
career.organization: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD)
Educational Council for Foreign Medical Graduates

narrative_blocks.header: NICHD branch chief, HEW Superior Service Medal 1971
narrative_blocks.body: Vassar then Johns Hopkins MD then Rochester residency. She served as assistant director of Strong Memorial Hospital before becoming branch chief and assistant director of the Office of Program Planning and Evaluation at NICHD. The Department of Health Education and Welfare awarded her its Superior Service Medal in 1971.

narrative_blocks.header: Married NIH director Knutti at family summer home
narrative_blocks.body: After her first marriage to a Dr. Hardwicke ended in divorce, she married Dr. Ralph Eddy Knutti — then head of extramural programs at NIH's Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases Institute and soon to direct the National Heart Institute — on 17 August 1957 at the Hooker summer home in Blandford MA, with the Rev. Edwin Crocker officiating.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in Coast Guard Auxiliary, retired 1993
narrative_blocks.body: She served 25 years in the U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary qualifying in search and rescue and communications, retiring in 1993 at age 80. She and Ralph were buried together at Arlington National Cemetery — the only Hookers in the tree interred there.

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — alongside his wife Dr. Sarah Hooker Knutti

education.school_name: Western Reserve University School of Medicine

career.organization: University of Rochester School of Medicine
NIH National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases
career.notes: Commissioned in Public Health Service officer corps 1951; pioneered fellowship and grant programs at the new Institute.

career.organization: NIH National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases

career.notes: Director 1961-1965 — one of NIH's two largest Institutes. Asked by Dr. James Shannon to move from Arthritis Institute to head NHI.

narrative_blocks.body: After the 1951 Omnibus Medical Research Bill created three new NIH Institutes, he was asked to come to Bethesda to help build the National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases as its first Chief of Extramural Programs. He pioneered the fellowship and grant programs that became the model for NIH-wide extramural research support.

In 1961 NIH director Dr. James Shannon asked him to move to direct the National Heart Institute — now the National Heart, Lung and Blood Institute. He led one of NIH's two largest Institutes for four years before stepping down in 1965.

burialCemetery.notes: Longmeadow Cemetery in the long-time Hooker family town of Longmeadow, Hampden County MA. Burial site of Arthur Bowles Hooker (HD04885), son of Richard Hooker the Springfield Republican editor.
